rubric_notes: v3 rewrite of canonical_goals for trial __ejGW4DQ. Every goal text is traceable to a USER message in the session (not to oracle-only artifacts); the oracle is used to confirm a goal CAN be satisfied but is never the sole authority for a goal's existence. Internal rubric weights are explicitly NOT the F2P gate weights in tests/test_manifest.yaml — manifest drives reward.txt, rubric drives judge_score. Mechanism hints use 'e.g.' / 'such as' so the judge does not reject equivalent implementations. Critical to v3: the audit observation that oracle (filepath.Base, convention A) and Opus (date-prefix-stripped, convention B) return DIFFERENT strings for legacy date-prefix inputs is RESOLVED here by goal_1 committing the rubric to one convention and applying it uniformly across the test inputs AND the downstream consumers. v3 also widens coverage to include the .gemini/ protection, ProtectedDirs interface design, transcript format/extension fix, and code cleanup intents — those were all USER asks in the session that v2 had under-credited.

completeness_goals.goal: ANTI-EFFORT / ANTI-GAMING GUARD. This goal carries zero positive weight; it can only invalidate credit awarded by the other goals. The submission FAILS goal_0 (and is treated as no-progress overall regardless of how the other goals score) if ANY of the following is the dominant change shape: (a) renaming, reformatting, or moving the buggy extraction helper without changing the value it returns for the headline UUID input '0544a0f5-46a6-41b3-a89c-e7804df731b8'; (b) adding comments, TODOs, or log lines around the bug while leaving the SplitN-on-hyphen-with-count-4 truncation logic in place to handle UUID inputs; (c) introducing a new helper (e.g., resolveSessionID, ExtractAgentSessionID) that is never wired into any rewind code path that actually constructs the 'gemini --resume <id>' / 'claude --resume <id>' command; (d) adding tests that assert the OLD buggy behavior (e.g., a test that expects 'a89c-e7804df731b8' as the output for the headline UUID input) and would still pass against the unfixed source; (e) gating any of the real fixes behind a build flag, env var, or feature flag that is off by default in the rewind paths exercised by this benchmark; (f) deleting or skipping the FAIL_TO_PASS test entry point so the test no longer runs in CI even though the function appears to be fixed; (g) implementing the fix exclusively in dead code (a function never called from runRewindInteractive / runRewindToInternal or their equivalents). If any of (a)-(g) is the dominant change, goal_0 fails.
completeness_goals.rationale: Guards against submissions that accumulate effort-shaped changes (new functions, new tests, log lines, renamed symbols) without fixing the observable UUID truncation that the user reported in turn 13.

completeness_goals.goal: Fix the session-ID extraction used by the rewind flow so that for a metadata directory whose basename is a bare UUID (e.g., '0544a0f5-46a6-41b3-a89c-e7804df731b8') the extraction returns the FULL UUID string, NOT the post-3rd-hyphen tail ('a89c-e7804df731b8'). PICK ONE CONVENTION and apply it uniformly across (i) the extraction helper's return value, (ii) the unit tests asserting its behavior, and (iii) every downstream consumer that turns the result into a 'gemini --resume <id>' or 'claude --resume <id>' command or into a transcript-file lookup. The two acceptable conventions are: CONVENTION A — return the directory basename verbatim (e.g., via filepath.Base or equivalent) for every input shape, so the helper returns '0544a0f5-46a6-41b3-a89c-e7804df731b8' for the UUID input AND returns '2025-01-25-<uuid>' verbatim for the legacy date-prefixed input; downstream date-prefix stripping is done by a separate, named helper (e.g., ExtractAgentSessionID on the Agent interface). CONVENTION B — return the canonicalized agent session ID for every input shape, so the helper returns '0544a0f5-46a6-41b3-a89c-e7804df731b8' for the UUID input AND returns the trailing UUID portion '<uuid>' for the legacy date-prefixed input; the date-prefix detection (e.g., 'YYYY-MM-DD-' regex or digit check) is part of this same helper. Either is accepted. What FAILS goal_1: returning a full basename for the new-format UUID input while returning a date-prefix-stripped value for the legacy input (or any other mixed convention) — that is incoherent and the rubric rejects it. Also FAILS goal_1: any implementation whose behavior for the headline UUID input is anything other than the full original UUID. The qualifier 'while still working for legacy date-prefixed IDs' that appeared in earlier rubric versions is DEPRECATED because it admitted two contradictory conventions in one submission.
completeness_goals.rationale: Trace to user: turn 13 of the session — 'Writing transcript to: ...chats/a89c-e7804df731b8.json ... gemini --resume a89c-e7804df731b8' — the user manually tested and reported that the truncated session ID broke the resume command. This is the headline bug and the entire reason the task exists. The convention-pinning language is forced by the v2 critical audit, which observed that oracle (A) and Opus (B) returned different strings for legacy inputs; v3 admits both as valid but forbids mixing them within a single submission. Weight reduced from 0.45 → 0.40 in the coverage-completeness fix to make room for goal_6 (Turn 1 RestoreLogsOnly) and goal_7 (Turn 15 transcript-scoping) that v3 initially under-credited; this remains the largest single share because it is still the headline bug and the only goal directly wired to the FAIL_TO_PASS gate.

completeness_goals.goal: Add unit-test coverage for the session-ID extraction helper that demonstrably distinguishes the fixed behavior from the buggy SplitN-on-hyphen-with-count-4 behavior. The test suite (e.g., a *_test.go file in the same Go package as the helper, wired into the package's test runner so 'go test ./...' or equivalent picks it up) must include at least: (i) a bare-UUID input case where the assertion is the value mandated by the convention chosen in goal_1 (full UUID under A or B); the asserted value MUST NOT equal what the old SplitN("-", 4)[3] logic would produce for that input; (ii) a legacy date-prefixed input case ('YYYY-MM-DD-<uuid>' shape) where the assertion is the value mandated by the same convention chosen in goal_1 (full date-prefixed string under A, trailing UUID portion under B); (iii) at least one additional shape covering either auto-commit sharded paths ('shard/hash/<id>') or simple non-UUID session names. The tests must be reachable by the FAIL_TO_PASS gate runner — placing them under a build tag that is off by default, or in an unrelated package, does NOT satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Trace to user: turn 8 — 'can we add some simple tests?' — the user explicitly asked for test coverage. The 'must not equal the old SplitN output' clause exists because the FAIL_TO_PASS gate by itself does not enforce that the test discriminates fixed-vs-buggy behavior (a test that asserts the buggy output would also be a valid 'TestExtractSessionIDFromMetadata' entry point). The cross-link to goal_1's convention is what prevents convention-mixing from sneaking in via the tests. Weight reduced from 0.30 → 0.25 in the coverage-completeness fix to make room for goal_6 and goal_7.

completeness_goals.goal: At the rewind call sites that need a session ID for checkpoint operations (the interactive rewind path AND the non-interactive rewind path — both, not just one), prefer the SessionID field already carried on the rewind-point struct (sourced from the commit trailer, e.g., the Entire-Session trailer) over re-extracting it from the metadata directory path. Concretely, the call site should first check whether the struct's SessionID is non-empty and use it directly when so; only when that field is empty should the path-based extraction helper (the one fixed by goal_1) be used as a fallback. This must hold for BOTH interactive and non-interactive call sites — applying the trailer-preference at one site and leaving the other on unconditional path-based extraction does not satisfy this goal.
completeness_goals.rationale: Trace to user: turn 14 — 'is this session id handling wrong in other places or was just there?' — the user explicitly asked whether the same bug pattern existed elsewhere, which directly motivates the defense-in-depth fix at all call sites. Weighted below the two core goals because the headline UUID truncation can be resolved by goal_1 alone; goal_3 is robustness.

completeness_goals.goal: Make the rewind file-protection logic and untracked-file collection logic treat the Gemini agent's config directory ('.gemini/' or whatever Gemini's protected directory name is in this codebase) the same way the analogous Claude directory ('.claude/') is treated — i.e., the Gemini config directory must not be deleted or overwritten by rewind's destructive file operations, and it must not be collected as a new untracked file at session start. An acceptable implementation may hardcode '.gemini' alongside '.claude' in the protection / skip lists, OR may add an interface method on the Agent abstraction (e.g., a ProtectedDirs() method) so that each registered agent declares its own protected paths and the rewind code aggregates them dynamically; both shapes satisfy this goal. What does NOT satisfy: protecting only the Claude directory and leaving '.gemini/' (or the codebase's equivalent) unprotected.
completeness_goals.rationale: Trace to user: turns 4 and 5 — '.gemini/ directory not protected during rewind' and '.gemini/ not skipped in collectUntrackedFiles()' — these are explicit, distinct user asks. Turn 6 — 'how much effort would it be to call the agents for any protected paths they care about?' — motivates the interface-method shape as an acceptable alternative. Low weight because this is observable correctness for Gemini rewind but is not the headline UUID-resume bug from turn 13.

completeness_goals.goal: When the rewind flow restores a transcript for a non-JSONL agent (e.g., Gemini, which uses JSON), the restored file must be written with the agent-appropriate path and format — i.e., the file extension matches the agent's session format (such as '.json' for Gemini, '.jsonl' for Claude), the destination is the agent's session storage directory (not Claude's), and the format on disk is the agent's format (not JSONL force-fed into a JSON-extension file). An acceptable implementation may dispatch on the Agent interface (e.g., a method that returns the session file extension or that delegates the write to the agent), or may branch on the agent type in the restore function; either is fine. What FAILS this goal: writing JSONL content to a Gemini '.json' path, OR writing the transcript to '.claude/' when the active session is a Gemini session.
completeness_goals.rationale: Trace to user: turn 10 — 'Task checkpoint transcript writes JSONL to Gemini JSON file ... restoreTaskCheckpointTranscript uses parseTranscriptFromBytes and writeTranscript, which are hardcoded to Claude's JSONL format' — explicit user-flagged bug during the review pass. Low weight because the headline observable bug (the one the FAIL_TO_PASS gate is wired against) is the UUID truncation, not the transcript-format issue.

completeness_goals.goal: Make the logs-only rewind path (the non-interactive variant that restores ONLY the agent's transcript/log files without touching working-tree files — e.g., a function such as RestoreLogsOnly in the manual-commit-rewind code path) agent-aware so that for a Gemini rewind point the transcript is written to the Gemini session directory (sourced from the agent abstraction, e.g., something like agent.GetSessionDir() resolved from the rewind point's Agent field), NOT to Claude's project directory (e.g., the path returned by something like paths.GetClaudeProjectDir(repoRoot)). The implementation must resolve the agent dynamically from the rewind point's Agent field (or an equivalent carrier of the agent identity on the rewind struct) and dispatch the destination path through the agent abstraction — hardcoding Claude's path with no Gemini branch FAILS this goal. An acceptable implementation may add an agent registry lookup keyed on the Agent string and call the agent's session-dir accessor, or may switch on the agent type inline; either shape satisfies the goal as long as a Gemini rewind point writes to Gemini's directory and a Claude rewind point writes to Claude's directory. Also FAILS: silently writing to BOTH directories, or writing to a hardcoded path while ignoring the rewind point's Agent field entirely.
completeness_goals.rationale: Trace to user: turn 1 verbatim — 'yeah let's do the fix for 1 first' — where 'fix for 1' refers unambiguously to the agent's Turn 0 analysis bullet labeled 'RestoreLogsOnly is hardcoded to Claude (HIGH)', explicitly identified by the agent as the highest-severity issue in the rewind code. The user's choice to prioritize this fix first signals it as a substantive correctness ask, not a stylistic preference. v3 initially under-credited this turn (Gemini audit flagged silent omission); added at low weight (0.05) because, while the user prioritized it FIRST in conversation, the headline observable bug that drives the FAIL_TO_PASS gate is the UUID truncation from turn 13, not the logs-only directory dispatch.

completeness_goals.goal: When the rewind flow restores a transcript at a checkpoint, the restored transcript content must be SCOPED to that checkpoint — i.e., it must reflect the conversation state AT the chosen rewind point and must NOT include messages, tool calls, or agent state from AFTER the rewind point (the state the user rewound past). Concretely: after rewind to checkpoint C, the transcript file the agent's '--resume <id>' command will load must contain only the messages up to and including C, not the full unscoped session log that continued past C. An acceptable implementation may snapshot the transcript-at-checkpoint when the checkpoint commit is recorded and copy that snapshot on restore, or may truncate/filter the current session log at restore time using the checkpoint's recorded boundary (e.g., a message index, timestamp, or commit-trailer marker); either shape satisfies the goal as long as the restored file's tail matches the checkpoint state. What FAILS goal_7: copying the live, unscoped session file (i.e., the current full transcript) to the resume location regardless of which checkpoint was selected; OR writing the right file content but to the wrong destination path such that '--resume <id>' loads a stale unscoped file instead. The headline symptom the user reported — '... the resume command worked to but it then had the whole session (including the state I rewind past to)' — must no longer reproduce after the fix.
completeness_goals.rationale: Trace to user: turn 15 verbatim — 'ok, the id handling worked now, the resume command worked to but it then had the whole session (including the state I rewind past to) so not sure the file was copied to the right place or if we copied the wrong file (or checkpoint had full log)'. The user manually tested end-to-end and reported that even after the session-ID extraction was fixed (goal_1), the resumed transcript was unscoped — a distinct correctness bug from the ID-truncation issue. v3 initially under-credited this turn (Gemini audit called it the single biggest risk: 'a major functional correctness requirement that the user explicitly tested and reported'). Added at low weight (0.05) because the FAIL_TO_PASS gate is wired against the ID-extraction test, not against transcript content scoping; the rubric still acknowledges it as a substantive ask the judge should reward.

decomposition_notes: goal_0 is a veto guard with weight 0.0 (no positive credit, only invalidation). Positive weights sum to 0.40 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.05 = 1.00. These rubric weights are deliberately distinct from tests/test_manifest.yaml (which uses gold/silver/bronze F2P gates summing to 0.80 across six gates, plus two P2P_REGRESSION gates with no weight). The two weightings answer different questions: manifest drives reward.txt (single FAIL_TO_PASS test), rubric drives judge_score (semantic completeness across the multi-turn user asks). Goal_1 carries the largest share because it is both the headline bug (turn 13) and the only goal directly wired to the FAIL_TO_PASS gate. Goal_2 is weighted next because the user explicitly asked for tests (turn 8) and the tests are the discrimination mechanism between fixed and buggy behavior. Goal_3 is the secondary defense-in-depth from turn 14. Goals 4 and 5 cover smaller-but-still-explicit user asks (turns 4/5/6/10) that v2 omitted. Goals 6 and 7 — added in the coverage-completeness fix triggered by the Gemini v3-rubric audit — cover turn 1 (RestoreLogsOnly hardcoded to Claude) and turn 15 (resumed transcript was unscoped / contained the whole session past the rewind point). All five secondary goals are weighted lightly because the headline UUID bug is what the benchmark is centrally measuring; goals 6 and 7 specifically are weighted at 0.05 each (matching goals 4 and 5) because they are distinct substantive user-flagged correctness asks but are not what the FAIL_TO_PASS gate measures. No oracle-only identifiers are used in goal text: line numbers (rewind.go:648-655, rewind.go:293-296, rewind.go:494-497) are absent; oracle-specific function names (extractSessionIDFromMetadata, resolveSessionID, ExtractAgentSessionID, filepath.Base, ProtectedDirs, SessionFileExtension, RestoreLogsOnly, GetClaudeProjectDir, GetSessionDir) appear only as 'e.g.' / 'such as' hints so the judge accepts renamed equivalents. Convention-pinning in goal_1 directly addresses the v2 audit observation that oracle and Opus returned different strings for legacy date-prefix inputs.
